title: Iguazu Falls: The Most Powerful Waterfall on Earth
summary: BZ Travel visits Iguazu Falls on the border of Brazil and Argentina — the most powerful waterfall on Earth with 275 individual falls. They experience it from every angle: the panoramic Brazilian side with its gradual reveal, the lookout platforms that put you inside the water, a helicopter flight revealing the full scale, the raw Argentine side with Devil's Throat, the lower circuit's jungle-framed views, and a speedboat ride straight into the falls.

english.heading: Introduction
english.text: There are places in this world that silence you the moment you arrive. Iguazu Falls is one of them. We showed it from above, from the water, from both sides of the border — Argentine and Brazilian. Every single angle left us completely speechless. This is the most powerful waterfall on Earth.

english.heading: Arriving at the Brazilian Side
english.text:
A short flight from Buenos Aires brings us to the jungle border of Brazil and Argentina. Brazilian side first. We just spotted the falls. And wow, that's all I can say. It's incredible.

Friends told us Iguazu Falls was something else entirely, something no other waterfall on Earth could match. We smiled, we nodded, and quietly thought: how different can a waterfall really be? We had seen Niagara. We had stood at the misty edge of Victoria Falls. We arrived here fully expecting something roughly the same. Then we took our first look — and everything we had assumed collapsed in an instant. We had been completely wrong.

english.heading: The Brazilian Trail
english.text:
The trail is just a little bit down, a little bit up. It's not a hard walk at all, and knowing there's an elevator at the end does help. The Brazilian side offers a gradual reveal of the falls. Each bend in the path exposes something new, building slowly and deliberately toward a full panoramic view.

The biggest surprise? The sheer overwhelming scale of it all. We expected one waterfall — a single dramatic drop. What we found was 275 individual falls scattered across the jungle in every direction, as far as the eye could see. Every bend delivers a view that looks almost too beautiful to be real. A mist hangs permanently in the distance. The roar grows louder with every single step.

Along the lower trail, every step brings something bigger, something louder. Hundreds of falls side by side, each one powerful enough to headline any destination on Earth. Nothing — not a photograph, not a travel blog, not a single video — prepares you for what Iguazu actually is.

english.heading: The Lookout Platforms
english.text:
We're walking faster because we've spotted some lookout platforms right over the waterfall. Everybody's rushing.

At other waterfalls, you stand across. You find your spot, you look, and the waterfall performs for you — you are the audience. At Niagara, you watch from across a river. At Victoria, from across a gorge. At Iguazu, there is no "across." This place was built around one radical idea: do not show people the waterfall — put them in it.

The bridge takes you into the water, not around it. You are walking out over the actual river, water flowing beneath your feet, falls surrounding you on multiple sides, spray hitting you from every angle. The sound is no longer around you — it is inside you. You are wet. You are surrounded. You are, by any reasonable definition, inside a waterfall.

english.heading: The Helicopter Ride
english.text:
Right next to the elevator at the bottom is a spectacular view looking straight toward this massive waterfall. It's overwhelming.

The helicopter ride was surprisingly affordable — just over $100. From above, the first thing that hits you is the jungle: endless in every direction. And right in the middle, on the border of Brazil and Argentina, the falls. From the ground, you collect pieces. From the air, you finally see the whole — 3 km of falls, jungle pressing in from every side.

Up here, you see exactly where the calm river ends and the chaos begins. Below is the Argentine side with the long walkway leading to Devil's Throat — 14 of the most powerful falls converging at a single point, plunging 82 meters straight down. It looks less like a waterfall and more like the Earth simply swallowing the river whole.

If you're coming to Iguazu, do the helicopter ride. It is genuinely something special.

english.heading: Day Two: The Argentine Side
english.text:
This is our second day at Iguazu Falls, and today we're on the Argentine side. This is two cans. This is the central station and we're going to be getting on the train.

The Argentine side is bigger — significantly bigger. A small park train runs two stops. The second brings you directly to the catwalk for Devil's Throat. That is where we are going first. It's a 1.1-kilometer walk — about 10 to 15 minutes.

The Brazilian side gives you one path, one descent, one breathtaking panoramic view. The Argentine side gives you something rawer: a kilometer of elevated walkways crossing from island to island above the river, the jungle pressing in, water moving fast beneath your feet.

english.heading: Devil's Throat
english.text:
You hear it first — a rumble, low, distant, felt more than heard — while you're still crossing the catwalks above water that is perfectly, almost eerily calm. The river does not know yet what it is walking into.

Then you arrive at the edge, and everything you thought you understood about waterfalls dissolves. The water curves into a vast, terrifying horseshoe and plunges nearly 300 feet into a void that gives back nothing but thunder and white mist. It looks like the Earth has a drain — like someone pulled the plug and the entire river is being swallowed whole.

Unbelievable. Millions of gallons going over that edge every single second. This is Devil's Throat: the crown jewel, the reason for all of it. Raw, deafening, alive in a way that borders on frightening. You do not admire it — you stand inside it, soaked through, and you let it happen to you.

english.heading: The Lower Circuit
english.text:
The train takes us to the lower circuit — waterfalls at every turn, ending at a viewpoint toward Saint Martin's Island, a true Iguazu highlight. Every time you think you have seen the best, Iguazu surprises you.

Deep in the jungle, the views stop making sense. Jungle in the foreground, a curtain of white water over the cliff, framed so perfectly it looks designed. If you had to illustrate paradise, this is the picture.

The walkways take you right to the edge — the actual reach where the water gathers itself before going over the cliff. You're not watching from across. You are standing in the middle of it. Some things exist beyond description. This is one of them.

english.heading: The Speedboat Ride
english.text:
Saint Martin's Fall — one of Iguazu's two most iconic spots. On the edge, thousands of gallons drop into mist so thick there is no visible bottom. Spectacular from the top, but we are heading underneath.

We board the jungle bus for the most thrilling part of the visit: a speedboat ride straight into the falls. This is not a scenic cruise. The boat hammers against the current at full speed, throwing you over rapids that feel more like a theme park ride than a river ride.

For the full experience, take the front of the boat or the elevated back row. The boat drives straight into the curtain of falling water and the world disappears: just noise, white water, cold, hitting like a wall. The boat pulls back. You gasp. The boat turns and goes straight back in. Completely soaked, completely, absolutely, undeniably alive.

english.heading: Closing Thoughts
english.text:
Iguazu Falls — of everything we have seen, everything we have experienced, this is it. The one that sits above all others with nothing close behind. If it is not on your bucket list, it needs to be. You will not regret it.

We hope you felt even a fraction of what we felt standing here. Tell us what you think in the comments — we would love to hear from you. Thank you for watching.

Our next stop is Rio de Janeiro, the jewel of South America. Beaches, mountains, Christ the Redeemer watching over it all. One of the most beautiful cities on Earth. See you in the next video.

chinese.heading: 與眾不同的瀑布
chinese.text: 世界上有些地方，你一到達就會被震懾得說不出話來。伊瓜蘇瀑布就是其中之一。橫跨巴西與阿根廷的邊界，這是地球上最強大的瀑布。從布宜諾斯艾利斯搭乘短程航班，就能抵達這片叢林邊境，275條瀑布朝各個方向散布在雨林中。朋友們曾說伊瓜蘇是截然不同的存在——沒有任何瀑布能與之匹敵。去過尼加拉瀑布、也站過維多利亞瀑布霧氣瀰漫的邊緣，他們抵達時心裡想的大概差不多就是那樣。然而當他們親眼一看，所有預設的想法瞬間瓦解。

chinese.heading: 巴西側：全景式的登場
chinese.text: 巴西側提供了一種漸進式的瀑布展現。小徑的每個彎道都展現新的景象，緩慢而刻意地鋪陳，最終迎來完整的全景視野。最大的驚喜是那壓倒性的規模。他們原本預期會看到一個單一的壯觀水幕，結果卻是275條瀑布散布在叢林中，綿延到視線的盡頭。遠處薄霧終年瀰漫，轟隆聲隨著每一步越來越響亮。小徑持續向下延伸，瀑布一層一層地展現自己。沒有任何事物——不是照片、不是旅遊部落格、不是任何影片——能讓你真正準備好面對伊瓜蘇的真實面貌。

chinese.heading: 走進瀑布之中
chinese.text: 在其他瀑布，你站在對面。你找好位置，看著瀑布為你表演。你是觀眾。那是宏偉的劇場，但終究只是劇場。在尼加拉，你隔著河流觀看。在維多利亞，你隔著峽谷觀看。但在伊瓜蘇，沒有「對面」這種事。橋樑不會帶你繞過水、或在安全距離之上通過——它帶你走進水裡。你走在真正的河流上方，水流在腳下流動，瀑布從四面八方包圍你，水花從每個角度襲來。聲音不再環繞你——它在你的體內。你和體驗之間沒有任何欄杆。用任何合理的定義來說，你都在一座瀑布之中。

chinese.heading: 惡魔之喉：核心景點
chinese.text: 阿根廷側更大——明顯大得多。園區小火車直接帶遊客前往惡魔之喉的棧道，這是整個伊瓜蘇系統最具戲劇性的核心景點。一公里長的高架步道越過一個個小島，橫跨河流之上，叢林從兩側逼近，腳下水流湍急。在惡魔之喉，14條最強大的瀑布匯聚於一點，垂直落下82公尺。水流彎成一個巨大的、令人恐懼的馬蹄形，消失在虛空之中，只傳來雷鳴和白色水霧。它看起來與其說是瀑布，更像是地球直接把整條河流吞了下去。每秒鐘數百萬加侖的水從邊緣傾瀉而下。你不是在欣賞它——你站在它裡面，全身濕透，任由它衝擊你。

chinese.heading: 空中與水上的視角
chinese.text: 從空中俯瞰，直升機之旅展現了全貌。三公里長的瀑布在你腳下延伸，叢林從四面八方逼近。在地面上，你收集的是碎片。從空中，你終於看見整體。但最令人興奮的部分是當他們搭上快艇的時候。這不是一趟風景遊船——船隻以全速逆流衝擊，將你拋過急流。然後它直接駛入傾瀉而下的水幕之中，整個世界隨之消失。只剩下噪音、白水、和像牆一樣撞擊過來的冰冷。腎上腺素在海水衝擊的瞬間引爆，讓你徹底地、絕對地、無可否認地感受到活著。

collocations.meaning: 讓某人說不出話來（震驚到無法言語）
collocations.example: There are places in this world that silence you the moment you arrive.
collocations.example_zh: 世界上有些地方，你一抵達就會被震懾到說不出話。
collocations.practice: The view from the mountaintop ______ (讓我們說不出話).

collocations.meaning: 壓倒性的規模；極其龐大的尺度
collocations.example: The biggest surprise was the sheer overwhelming scale of it all.
collocations.example_zh: 最大的驚喜是那壓倒性的規模。
collocations.practice: Visitors are often shocked by the ______ (龐大規模) of the Grand Canyon.

collocations.phrase: as far as the eye can see
collocations.meaning: 一望無際；視線所及之處
collocations.example: 275 individual falls scattered across the jungle as far as the eye could see.
collocations.example_zh: 275條瀑布散布在叢林中，一望無際。
collocations.practice: The desert stretched out ______ (一望無際).
collocations.practice_answer: as far as the eye could see

collocations.example: The jungle pressing in, water moving fast beneath your feet.
collocations.example_zh: 叢林從兩側逼近，腳下水流湍急。
collocations.practice: During the parade, the crowd was ______ (從四面八方湧來).

collocations.example: The roar is raw, deafening, alive in a way that borders on frightening.
collocations.example_zh: 那轟鳴聲原始、震耳欲聾、充滿生命力，近乎令人害怕。
collocations.practice: His obsession with perfection ______ (近乎病態).

collocations.example: Some things exist beyond description. This is one of them.
collocations.example_zh: 有些事情存在於言語之外。這就是其中之一。
collocations.practice: The beauty of the sunset was ______ (難以形容).

collocations.example: The boat hammers against the current at full speed.
collocations.example_zh: 船隻以全速逆流衝擊。
collocations.practice: Waves ______ (猛烈拍打) the rocky shore during the storm.

collocations.meaning: 像牆一樣迎面襲來（形容衝擊力極強）
collocations.example: Noise, white water, cold, hitting like a wall.
collocations.example_zh: 噪音、白水、和像牆一樣撞擊過來的冰冷。
collocations.practice: When he walked outside, the heat ______ (像牆一樣襲來).

grammar.point: 分詞構句 — V-ing 開頭表伴隨情況
grammar.explanation: 分詞構句（Participle Phrase）可以將兩個相關的動作或狀態合併成一句，讓句子更簡潔優雅。當主句和附屬句的主詞相同時，附屬句的動詞改為 V-ing 形式。

examples.from_text: Having seen Niagara and stood at the misty edge of Victoria Falls, they arrived expecting something roughly the same.
examples.analysis:
→ 'Having seen...' = After they had seen... （完成式分詞構句，強調先發生）
→ 'expecting...' = and they expected... （簡單分詞構句，表伴隨狀態）
examples.pattern: Having + p.p. + ..., S + V ... → "完成某事之後，主詞做..."

examples.from_text: The boat hammers against the current, throwing you over rapids.
examples.analysis:
→ 'throwing you over rapids' = and it throws you over rapids
→ 表伴隨結果：主動作造成的副效果
examples.pattern: S + V + O, V-ing + O ... → "主詞做某事，因而產生..."

grammar.practice: ______ (走進房間), she noticed something strange on the table.
grammar.practice_answer: Walking into the room / Having walked into the room

grammar.point: 比較結構 — 否定詞 + 比較級 (Nothing / No... could...)
grammar.explanation: 用否定詞（nothing, no, never）+ 比較級，可以表達「沒有什麼比...更...」的超級最高級意味，比直接使用最高級更有強調效果。

examples.from_text: Nothing — not a photograph, not a travel blog, not a single video — prepares you for what Iguazu actually is.
examples.analysis:
→ 用三個 not 排比強調「沒有任何方式能讓你準備好」
→ 比直接說 'No one can prepare you' 更有感染力

examples.from_text: None of them come close.
examples.analysis:
→ 'none... come close' = 沒有任何一個能相提並論
→ 口語中極高頻的比較否定用法

grammar.practice: ______ (沒有什麼比賽) is more exciting than the World Cup final.
grammar.practice_answer: Nothing / No competition

grammar.point: 對比平行結構 — At X..., at Y..., at Z...
grammar.explanation: 使用三個以上平行的對比結構，可以強化差異、製造韻律感，是英文寫作中的華麗修辭手法（稱為「排比」或 parallel structure）。

examples.from_text: At Niagara, you watch from across a river. At Victoria, from across a gorge. At Iguazu, there is no across.
examples.analysis:
→ 三個 'At...' 開頭，前兩個描述其他瀑布的相似點
→ 第三個來個反轉（'there is no across'），達到強烈的對比效果

grammar.practice: In spring, the flowers bloom. In summer, ______ (太陽炙熱). In autumn, the leaves fall.
grammar.practice_answer: the sun burns hot

sentence_patterns.pattern: There are places in this world that + V...
sentence_patterns.meaning: 世界上有些地方...（強烈推薦的開場句）
sentence_patterns.example: There are places in this world that silence you the moment you arrive.
sentence_patterns.template: There are [places/things/people] in this world that [描述性動詞] you the moment you [動詞].
sentence_patterns.drill: There are ______ (世界上有些城市) that make you feel at home the moment you step off the plane.
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: cities in this world

sentence_patterns.pattern: No words are adequate for... / Some things exist beyond...
sentence_patterns.meaning: 無法用言語形容...（表達極致感受）
sentence_patterns.example: We wish we had better words. We do not. Some things exist beyond description.
sentence_patterns.template: No words are adequate for [名詞]. / Some things exist beyond [名詞].
sentence_patterns.drill: No words are adequate for ______ (她在婚禮上看起來有多美).
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: how beautiful she looked at the wedding

sentence_patterns.pattern: If it's not on your [名詞], it needs to be.
sentence_patterns.meaning: 如果它不在你的...清單上，它應該要在（強烈推薦某事物）
sentence_patterns.example: If it's not on your bucket list, it needs to be.
sentence_patterns.template: If [事物] is not on your [list/schedule/radar], it needs to be.
sentence_patterns.drill: If this restaurant ______ (不在你的口袋名單上), it needs to be.
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: is not on your list

sentence_patterns.pattern: The one that sits above all others with nothing close behind.
sentence_patterns.meaning: 遙遙領先所有其他事物，後無來者（最高級強調句）
sentence_patterns.example: The one that sits above all others with nothing close behind.
sentence_patterns.template: [事物] sits above all others with nothing close behind.
sentence_patterns.drill: In terms of customer service, this company ______ (遙遙領先，後無來者).
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: sits above all others with nothing close behind

sentence_patterns.pattern: You do not [動詞A] it — you [動詞B] it, and you let it [動詞] you.
sentence_patterns.meaning: 你不是在「做某事」它——你是...（否定→轉折強調的修辭手法）
sentence_patterns.example: You do not admire it. You stand inside it, soaked through, and you let it happen to you.
sentence_patterns.template: You do not [消極動詞] it — you [積極動詞] it, and you let it [動詞] you.
sentence_patterns.drill: You don't just watch the aurora — ______ (你感受它，讓它包圍你).
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: you experience it, and you let it surround you

quiz.q: What is the main purpose of this video?
choices: To compare Iguazu Falls with other famous waterfalls
To describe the experience of visiting Iguazu Falls from multiple perspectives
To explain how Iguazu Falls was formed geologically
To give practical travel tips for visiting Brazil and Argentina
quiz.explanation: The video shows Iguazu from every angle — the Brazilian side, the Argentine side, from the air in a helicopter, and from the water in a speedboat — all to convey the full experience. While it does compare Iguazu to Niagara and Victoria Falls, that comparison serves the larger goal of describing the experience.

quiz.q: What does the phrase 'there is no across' at Iguazu Falls mean?
choices: You cannot see the falls from the opposite side
The falls are too wide to see across
The walkways take you directly into the waterfall rather than letting you watch from a distance
The border between Brazil and Argentina blocks the view
quiz.explanation: The narrator explains: 'At other waterfalls, you stand across... at Iguazu, there is no across.' The bridge takes you into the water, not around it. You are 'inside a waterfall,' not watching from a distance.

quiz.q: The word 'sheer' in 'the sheer overwhelming scale' is closest in meaning to:
quiz.explanation: Here, 'sheer' means 'complete and total' — emphasizing the absolute, unqualified nature of the overwhelming scale. 'Sheer' is a common intensifier: sheer luck, sheer terror, sheer beauty.

quiz.q: How does the narrator create a sense of contrast between Iguazu and other waterfalls?
choices: By listing the heights of different waterfalls
By using a parallel structure: 'At Niagara... At Victoria... At Iguazu...'
By showing photographs of each waterfall
By interviewing other tourists about their experiences
quiz.explanation: The narrator uses a powerful parallel structure: 'At Niagara, you watch from across a river. At Victoria, from across a gorge. At Iguazu, there is no across.' This technique (排比) creates rhythm and dramatically emphasizes the unique nature of Iguazu.

quiz.q: Which sentence best captures the overall feeling the narrator wants to convey?
choices: Iguazu Falls is an affordable travel destination.
Iguazu Falls is an experience that cannot be fully described in words.
The Brazilian side is better than the Argentine side.
The helicopter ride is the best part of the visit.
quiz.explanation: The narrator repeatedly emphasizes that words fall short: 'We wish we had better words. We do not. Some things exist beyond description. This is one of them.' The video's central message is that Iguazu must be experienced to be understood.

title: We Were Almost Entirely Wrong About China
summary: We Were Almost Entirely Wrong About China

english.heading: Part 1
english.text:
We had a theory about China. We had read
things, watched the news, talked to
people who had been there. We thought we
had a reasonable picture of what to
expect.
We were wrong. Not a little wrong,
almost entirely wrong. This is the first
video in a series about 22 days inside
China.
We traveled privately, just the two of
us, with a different local guide in each
city.
No tour buses, no group schedules, just
us moving through one of the most
extraordinary countries on earth trying
our best to understand it. We are going
to show you the landscapes and the food
and the things you come to China to see.
But we are also going to try to explain
something harder. What this country
actually is, how it thinks, what it has
been through, why it ended up here.
Because if you only watch the pretty
pictures, you will miss the whole story.
My name is Tresa. That guy you keep
seeing on screen exploring everything,
sitting in every car, that's my husband,
Zarko.
Together, we run this channel and
together we spent 22 days traveling
China privately.
We came back with more questions than
answers, which honestly is the best
possible outcome from any trip.
I'm American, born and raised in the US,
which means I grew up with a pretty
specific picture of the world.
Communism was the enemy. The Soviet
block was gray and scary.
And China was this distant, mysterious
place you only heard about when
something went wrong.
Zarko's story is different. He grew up
in Bulgaria under communism. Not reading
about it, living it. one party, state
control over everything. You kept your
head down and you did not ask the wrong
questions.
He left at 25 as a political refugee,
built a business in Seattle that
supported 125 families. And when we
retired, we moved back to Bulgaria to
Burgus on the Black Sea. That was 7
years ago.
I'm telling you all of this because it
matters for everything you're about to
see. When I look at China, I'm looking
through American eyes. When Zarko looks
at China, he's looking through the eyes
of someone who actually lived inside a
communist system.
Our questions are completely different.
And when we compare notes at the end of
the day, neither one of us has the full
picture alone, but together we get a
little closer to something real. That's
what this series is about. Getting
closer to something real about China.
Before we left, we spent weeks trying to
prepare,
reading, watching documentaries,
talking to anyone who'd actually been
there. And we kept coming back to the
sam

english.heading: Part 2
english.text:
tanbul has become one
of the great travel hubs in the world,
connected to almost everywhere. With
business class fairs that are a fraction
of what you'd pay on a European carrier.
On a 10-hour flight, business class
stops being a luxury. It becomes
self-preservation.
We landed early morning at Shanghai
Puong. And for our very first experience
of China, we decided to take the magv
into the city. Magnetic levitation, no
wheels on the track, top speed of 430
kmh.
It felt like exactly the right way to
arrive.
Honest review, it's 20 years old. The
technology is actually German, invented
in Germany, built here, then largely
abandoned worldwide because it was too
expensive and too inflexible to scale.
The train was nearly empty on a weekday
morning. The interior felt dated, and
the top speed we actually hit was around
300 km hour, which sounds impressive
until you learn that China's regular
high-speed trains now reach similar
speeds across the entire country. We'd
see that for ourselves on our first
bullet train ride a few days later. Our
hotel was right on Nanjing Road, not
near it, on it. We opened the curtains
and there it was, directly below us. The
most famous commercial street in
Shanghai, running from the bun straight
through the heart of the city. It never
stops, day or night. We slept for a few
hours. The 7-hour time difference from
Bulgaria is not kind. And then we went
walking.
This is the moment, the one I'd heard
other travelers talk about but hadn't
quite believed.
when what you expected and what you're
actually standing inside quietly come
apart.
The streets are spotless. The
architecture is extraordinary.
Every luxury brand you've ever heard of
is here
alongside Chinese brands I'd never
encountered.
People are well-dressed, unhurried,
going about their lives. Everything
moves with this purposeful efficiency. I
can only describe as organized.
People compare Nanjing Road at night to
Time Square. I've been to Time Square.
Time Square is loud and chaotic in a way
that feels almost accidental.
Nanjing Road feels deliberate, like
someone asked what a great city street
should actually look like and then built
the answer.
There's a robot that makes coffee right
there on the street. You walk up, tap
your order on a screen, and a mechanical
arm makes your espresso.
We walked to the Bund as the sun was
going down, the famous waterfront
prominade along the Hangpoo River with
grand colonial era European buildings on
one side and the futuristic towers of
Puong

english.heading: Part 3
english.text:
utright banned, considered a national
security risk.
In Europe, there's ongoing debate.
And then you walk into their flagship
store in Shanghai and you immediately
understand why they make governments
nervous.
This is not a phone company. Huawei is
an entire ecosystem.
Smartphones, tablets, laptops,
smartwatches, home automation, and cars.
Full electric vehicles, multiple models,
beautiful things. Zarko moved through
that showroom like a kid the week before
Christmas. He sat in every single one.
The ST9, the Z7, the M9, every one of
them.
What stopped us both was a laptop called
the Matebook Fold.
It opens like a normal laptop.
Then the screen unfolds into a fulls
size display.
Something between a tablet and a
workstation in a single piece of
hardware.
The engineering is genuinely remarkable.
Zarko stood at that table for a long
time.
Then the Xiaomi store. Xiaomi makes
everything. Phones, TVs, rice cookers,
scooters, robot vacuums,
and a car. The Xiaomi SU7 is an electric
sedan that's been making European and
American automakers quietly nervous.
It looks better than most of what it
competes with,
and it costs considerably less.
the DJI store. Everything you're
watching right now was filmed on a DJI
Osmo Pocket 4. Zarko's camera of choice
for this channel. Walking into the DJI
flagship felt a little ceremonial.
Walking store to store that morning,
something kept pulling at me. This isn't
innovation arriving from nowhere. China
graduates more engineers every year than
any other country on Earth. The
government identified AI, electric
vehicles, and advanced manufacturing as
national priorities and then sustained
that investment for decades. Whatever
you think of the political system behind
those decisions, the output is sitting
right in front of you and it works.
>> You can go 1,000 km on this 1,400
hybrid.
One of the most remarkable things we saw
in Shanghai wasn't a landmark or a tech
showroom. It was a supermarket. Hearesh,
owned by Alibaba, looks like an upscale
grocery at first. Seafood tanks where
you point at your fish and they prepare
it for you. Beautiful produce, a hot
food section, normal aisles. But look
up. Running above the entire store is a
conveyor belt. A whole network of
ceiling tracks with bags hanging from
them, moving in every direction. A
silent river of orders flowing overhead
while people shop below. HEA isn't just
a supermarket. It's a fulfillment center
that also lets people shop in person.
Employees walk the aisles filling online
o

english.heading: Part 4
english.text:
here,
a drone arrived. an actual drone
descending from above the buildings with
a food bag hanging underneath it,
landing precisely on a marked drop zone.
The food came from a restaurant on the
rooftop of a nearby mall. From the
moment someone placed the order to the
moment the drone landed, 12 minutes.
Our guide told us her mother, an elderly
woman living alone, gets almost
everything delivered this way.
groceries, meals, household items. She
orders on WeChat, pays on WeChat, and it
arrives at her door. She never has to
carry heavy bags or fight through
crowded markets. I found that genuinely
moving. And that's when I started to
understand why everyone in China is on
their phone. It isn't mindless
scrolling. Their phone isn't a
distraction from their life. It is the
system that runs their life. And in
China, it runs it remarkably well.
We also spent time in the BYD showroom.
Build your dreams.
Now the largest electric vehicle
manufacturer in the world by sales.
Bigger than Tesla.
Bigger than Volkswagen.
In China, electric vehicles aren't a
trend or a glimpse of the future.
They're just what cars are.
One of the malls we visited had car
dealerships on the ground floor. Inside
the shopping mall, not in the parking
lot, between the restaurant and the
clothing store. You could buy an
electric vehicle and then go up two
floors for dinner. The contrast with the
Tesla showroom nearby was hard to
ignore. Huawei had eight or nine models
filling the floor. And Neo is similar.
multiple models, lounge atmosphere,
salespeople who genuinely wanted to walk
you through everything. Tesla had two
cars. It felt sparse by comparison.
On our last morning in Shanghai, our
guide did something we weren't
expecting.
Instead of another landmark or another
showroom, she took us to a park. It was
early, maybe , in the morning,
and the park was full. Not full like a
tourist site. Full of people who belong
there. Retired people mostly spread
across the whole space in different
groups. Each one doing something
completely different.
>> What is the name of this park?
>> Pushing Park.
>> Pushing Park. Pushing Park.
>> Pushing Park.
>> Those are for disabled people.
>> Wow.
>> Tai Chi in one corner. a ballroom
dancing group nearby. 20 people partner
dancing on the paving stones to music
from a small speaker on a bench.
Further along, a group of musicians
playing traditional instruments
together.
And then further still, a group just
singing together like they did this
every single morning.

english.heading: Part 5
english.text:
fterwards that I
keep coming back to. He said, "This is
what retirement should look like." I
couldn't argue with him. After those
first few days in Shanghai, one question
kept coming back. We'd walked Nanjing
Road, stood at the Bund, watched a drone
deliver lunch in 12 minutes, danced with
strangers in a park at in the
morning. How did this happen? Here's
what I knew going in. In 1950, China was
one of the poorest countries on Earth.
The average person here earned less than
the average person in subsaharan Africa.
A century of war, invasion, famine, and
political upheaval had torn the country
apart. No electricity in most villages,
no paved roads, no real industry. And
then it got worse before it got better.
In 1958, Mao Zaong launched what he
called the Great Leap Forward. The plan
was to transform China into a modern
industrial power. Within 15 years,
private farming was abolished.
The people at home had nothing left to
eat. Between 15 and 45 million people
died in the famine that followed.
This is the China that existed within
living memory.
Many of the older people we met on this
trip, guides, hotel staff, people who
looked roughly our age, were born into
that world, or their parents were. Real
hollow stomach starvation isn't distant
history here. It's one generation back.
Then in 1978, something changed. Mao had
died two years earlier. A man named Dung
Xiaoing took power. And Dung looked at
the wreckage of the previous 30 years
and made a decision that was remarkable
for how simple it was. Whatever works,
he said, if a cat catches mice, it
doesn't matter if it's black or white.
He opened China to foreign investment,
allowed private business alongside the
state, created special zones where
market capitalism was explicitly
permitted just to test it, to see what
happened. He sent Chinese students
abroad to study in western universities.
He rewarded results. He kept the
communist party in political control,
kept the one party system, the state
surveillance, the control of
information. But onto all of that he
grafted a form of capitalism so
aggressive and competitive. It makes
most western economies look cautious.
And the result, China today is not
communist in any way Markx would
recognize. It's something with no clean
name. A one party political structure
running on top of an extraordinarily
dynamic market economy.
The government sets direction. The
market does the work. The people get
richer and in exchange for that rising
prosperity, they accept a leve

chinese.heading: 第一部分
chinese.text: 我們對中國有一套理論。我們讀過一些東西、看過新聞、也和去過那裡的人聊過。我們以為自己對即將面對的情況有合理的認識。我們錯了——不是小錯，而是幾乎全錯。這是一個關於我們在中國境內旅行22天的系列影片的第一集。我們以私人方式旅行，只有我們兩個人，每個城市都有不同的當地導遊陪同。沒有旅遊巴士，沒有團體行程，只有我們穿梭在這顆星球上最非凡的國度之一，盡全力去理解它。我們會帶你去看風景、品美食、看那些你來中國想看的一切。但我們也想試著解釋更難的東西——這個國家到底是什麼樣子、它如何思考、它經歷過什麼、為什麼走到了今天這一步。因為如果你只看漂亮的畫面，你就會錯過整個故事。我叫Tresa。你在螢幕上一直看到的那個探索一切、坐在每輛車裡的男人，是我的丈夫Zarko。我們一起經營這個頻道，一起在中國私人旅行了22天。我們帶回來的問題比答案多——說實話，這是一趟旅行最好的結果。我是美國人，在美國出生長大。這意味著我對世界有一套相當特定的認識：共產主義是敵人，蘇聯陣營是灰暗而可怕的，而中國則是個遙遠而神祕的地方，只有在出問題的時候才會聽說它。Zarko的故事不一樣。他在保加利亞的共產主義制度下長大——不是讀到而已，而是親身經歷。一黨專政、國家控制一切。你必須低調行事，不能問不該問的問題。他在25歲時以政治難民身份離開，後來在西雅圖創辦了一家養活125個家庭的企業。我們退休後搬回了保加利亞，住在黑海邊的布爾加斯。那是七年前的事了。我告訴你這些，是因為這對你即將看到的一切都很重要。當我看著中國時，我帶著一個美國人的眼光——

chinese.heading: 第二部分
chinese.text: 伊斯坦堡已經成為世界上最大的旅行樞紐之一，幾乎可以連接任何地方。商務艙的價格僅是歐洲航空公司的幾分之一。在十小時的飛行中，商務艙不再是一種奢侈品，而是一種自我保護。我們在清晨抵達上海浦東。作為對中國的第一個體驗，我們決定搭乘磁浮列車進城——磁力懸浮，軌道上沒有輪子，最高時速430公里。這是抵達這座城市最恰當的方式。老實說，這項技術已經有20年了。它其實是德國技術，在德國發明，在這裡建造，然後在全球大部分地區被放棄，因為它太昂貴、太缺乏彈性、難以規模化。列車在平日上午幾乎空無一人，內部裝潢感覺有點過時，我們實際達到的最高時速約為300公里——這聽起來很厲害，直到你發現中國的常規高鐵現在在全國範圍內都能達到類似的速度。我們幾天後第一次搭乘高鐵時就會親身體驗到。我們的酒店就在南京路上——不是靠近它，而是在它上面。我們拉開窗簾，它就在我們正下方——上海最著名的商業街道，從外灘一直延伸到城市的心臟。它從不停歇，日夜不分。我們睡了幾個小時。與保加利亞七小時的時差可不客氣。然後我們就出門散步了。這就是那個時刻——我曾聽其他旅行者說起過但不太相信的那個時刻——當你的預期和你實際上站立其中的現實靜靜地分道揚鑣的時候。街道一塵不染。建築令人嘆為觀止。所有你聽說過的奢侈品牌都在這裡，還有我從未見過的中國品牌。人們穿著得體，步調從容，過著自己的生活。一切都帶著一種我只能形容為「有條不紊」的高效節奏運轉。人們把夜晚的南京路比作——

chinese.heading: 第三部分
chinese.text: 被徹底禁止，被視為國家安全風險。在歐洲，這項爭論仍在持續。然後你走進它們在上海的旗艦店，立刻就明白為什麼它們讓各國政府感到不安。這不是一家手機公司。華為是一整個生態系統——智慧型手機、平板電腦、筆記型電腦、智慧手錶、家庭自動化設備，還有汽車。純電動車，多種型號，美輪美奐。Zarko在那個展廳裡走動的樣子就像聖誕節前一週的小孩，他每一輛都坐過了——ST9、Z7、M9，每一輛。真正讓我們倆都停下來的是一台叫做Matebook Fold的筆記型電腦。它像普通筆電一樣打開，然後螢幕展開成一個全尺寸顯示器——介於平板電腦和工作站之間的一體機。這項工程技術確實令人驚嘆。Zarko在那張桌子前站了很久。然後是小米專賣店。小米什麼都做——手機、電視、電鍋、滑板車、掃地機器人，還有一輛車。小米SU7是一款電動轎車，讓歐美汽車製造商暗自緊張。它比大多數競爭對手更好看，而且價格低得多。然後是大疆創新。你現在看到的一切都是用大疆Osmo Pocket 4拍攝的——Zarko為這個頻道選擇的相機。走進大疆旗艦店的感覺有點像朝聖。那天早上從一家店走到另一家店，有件事一直困擾著我：這不是憑空出現的創新。中國每年培養的工程師比世界上任何國家都多。政府將人工智慧、電動車和先進製造業定為國家優先事項，然後持續投入數十年。不管你對這些決策背後的政治體制怎麼看，產出就在你面前，而且它確實有效。這輛混合動力車可以跑1000公里。我們在上海看到的最了不起的事情之一不是地標建築，也不是科技展廳——

chinese.heading: 第四部分
chinese.text: 一架無人機出現在那裡——一架真正的無人機從建築物上方降下來，下面掛著一個餐袋，精準地降落在標記好的投放區。食物來自附近一座購物中心屋頂上的餐廳。從有人下單到無人機落地，總共12分鐘。我們的導遊告訴我們，她的母親——一位獨居的老太太——幾乎所有的東西都是用這種方式送達的：雜貨、餐點、日用品。她在微信上下單，用微信付款，東西就送到家門口。她再也不需要提重物或在擁擠的市場裡擠來擠去。我覺得這個故事真的很打動人。從那一刻起，我開始明白為什麼在中國每個人都盯著手機看。那不是無意識的滑動。他們的手機不是生活的干擾物——而是運行生活的系統。而在中國，它運行得非常好。我們還去了比亞迪的展廳——Build Your Dreams。它是現在全球銷量最大的電動車製造商，比特斯拉還大，比福斯還大。在中國，電動車不是一種趨勢或未來的預覽——它們就是汽車本來的樣子。我們去的一個購物中心，一樓就有汽車經銷商——在購物中心裡面，不是在停車場，而是在餐廳和服裝店之間。你可以買一輛電動車，然後上兩層樓去吃晚飯。這與附近特斯拉展廳的對比難以忽視。華為有八九個型號擺滿了整個樓層，蔚來也差不多——多種型號、 Lounge氛圍、真心想為你詳細介紹的銷售人員。特斯拉只有兩輛車。相比之下顯得空空蕩蕩。在上海的最後一個早晨，我們的導遊做了一件我們意想不到的事。她沒有帶我們去另一個地標或另一個展廳，而是帶我們去了公園。當時大概清晨，公園裡已經滿了——不是像旅遊景點那樣滿，而是滿了屬於那裡的人。退休的——

chinese.heading: 第五部分
chinese.text: 之後我一直想起他說的話。他說：「這就是退休生活應該有的樣子。」我無話可說。在上海那最初幾天之後，有一個問題一直縈繞心頭。我們走過南京路、站在外灘、看著無人機在12分鐘內送來午餐、清晨在公園裡和陌生人一起跳舞——這一切是怎麼發生的？這是我事先知道的：1950年，中國是地球上最貧窮的國家之一。這裡的人均收入低於撒哈拉以南非洲的人均水平。一個世紀的戰爭、侵略、饑荒和政治動盪將這個國家撕裂——大多數村莊沒有電、沒有鋪設道路、沒有真正的工業。然後情況變得更加糟糕。1958年，毛澤東發動了他所謂的「大躍進」，目標是將中國轉變為現代工業強國。15年內，私人農業被廢除。人民家裡沒有東西可吃。隨後的饑荒導致1500萬到4500萬人死亡。這就是存在於人們記憶中的中國。我們這趟旅行遇到的許多老年人——導遊、酒店員工、看起來和我們差不多年紀的人——都出生在那個世界，或者他們的父母是。真實的、飢腸轆轆的饑荒在這裡不是遙遠的歷史——它只隔了一代人。然後在1978年，情況發生了變化。毛澤東兩年前去世了。一個叫鄧小平的人掌握了權力。鄧小平看著過去30年的廢墟，做出了一個簡單到令人矚目的決定：「不管黑貓白貓，能捉老鼠的就是好貓。」他向外國投資開放了中國，允許私營企業與國營企業並存，創建了明確允許市場資本主義的經濟特區——只是為了測試它，看看會發生什麼。他派遣中國學生到西方大學留學。他獎勵成果。他讓共產黨繼續掌握政治權力——

collocations.meaning: 建立假設；形成先入為主的看法
collocations.example: We had built assumptions about China from news reports.
collocations.example_zh: 我們從新聞報導中對中國建立了種種假設。
collocations.practice: Before visiting a new country, it's easy to ______ from what you read online.

collocations.example: Our assumptions began to crumble the moment we arrived.
collocations.example_zh: 我們一抵達，我們的假設就開始崩塌。
collocations.practice: When I saw the real city, all my preconceptions started to ______.

collocations.example: Under communism, you kept your head down and did not ask the wrong questions.
collocations.example_zh: 在共產主義制度下，你必須低調行事，不要問不該問的問題。
collocations.practice: In a strict environment, it's often safer to ______ and follow the rules.

collocations.example: He left Bulgaria at 25 as a political refugee.
collocations.example_zh: 他在25歲時以政治難民的身份離開了保加利亞。
collocations.practice: Many people flee their home country as a ______ due to persecution.

collocations.meaning: 合理的認識；合理的畫面
collocations.example: We thought we had a reasonable picture of what to expect.
collocations.example_zh: 我們以為自己對即將面對的情況有合理的認識。
collocations.practice: After reading the guidebook, I felt I had a ______ of the city.

collocations.example: We came back with more questions than answers.
collocations.example_zh: 我們帶回了比答案更多的問題。
collocations.practice: The documentary left me with ______ about the political situation.

collocations.example: Zarko lived under one party and state control over everything.
collocations.example_zh: Zarko生活在一個政黨和國家控制一切的環境中。
collocations.practice: Under ______, the government decides what businesses can produce.

collocations.meaning: 錯過整個故事；未能理解全貌
collocations.example: If you only watch the pretty pictures, you will miss the whole story.
collocations.example_zh: 如果你只看漂亮的照片，你會錯過整個故事。
collocations.practice: If you judge a country only by its tourist attractions, you ______.

grammar.point: Past Perfect Tense for Background Information
grammar.explanation: 過去完成式用於描述在另一個過去事件之前已經發生的情況。在本影片中，「We had read things, watched the news... We thought we had a reasonable picture」使用過去完成式來強調在抵達中國之前就已經形成的認知。

examples.from_text: We had a theory about China. We had read things, watched the news, talked to people who had been there.
examples.analysis: had read, had watched, had talked — 這些過去完成式動詞描述在他們實際訪問中國之前所做的事情。主句使用簡單過去式（had a theory），而從句使用過去完成式來表示更早發生的動作。
examples.pattern: Subject + had + past participle + (object), before + subject + simple past...

examples.from_text: He grew up in Bulgaria under communism. Not reading about it, living it.
examples.analysis: 使用簡單過去式（grew up）敘述過去的事實。然後用現在分詞（reading, living）進行對比，強調親身經歷與間接了解的差異。
examples.pattern: Subject + simple past + ... Not + verb-ing + (about) + object, verb-ing + object

grammar.practice: 將以下句子改寫為過去完成式：'I read about the country before I visited it.'
grammar.practice_answer: 'I had read about the country before I visited it.'

grammar.point: Comparative Structures for Contrast
grammar.explanation: 比較結構用於對比兩個人物、觀點或經歷。影片中頻繁使用比較來凸顯Tresa和Zarko之間不同的背景和視角。

examples.from_text: Zarko's story is different. He grew up in Bulgaria under communism.
examples.analysis: 使用 'is different' 引入對比。然後詳細解釋Zarko的經歷如何與Tresa的經歷形成對比。這是一種常見的篇章組織方式。
examples.pattern: A's story/narrative/experience is different. A + verb + (details of difference)...

examples.from_text: I'm American... which means I grew up with a pretty specific picture of the world.
examples.analysis: 使用 'which means' 引入解釋或結果。這裡用來解釋Tresa的成長背景如何塑造了她的世界觀。
examples.pattern: Subject + be + nationality/identity, which means + subject + grew up with + noun phrase

grammar.practice: 完成句子：'My experience was different from my friend's. I grew up in a small town, which means ________.'
grammar.practice_answer: 'My experience was different from my friend's. I grew up in a small town, which means I had a simpler view of the world.'

sentence_patterns.pattern: We thought we had a [adjective] picture of [noun]
sentence_patterns.meaning: 我們以為自己對某事有某種認識
sentence_patterns.example: We thought we had a reasonable picture of what to expect.
sentence_patterns.template: We thought we had a [clear/reasonable/accurate] picture of [the situation/the country/what was coming].
sentence_patterns.drill: 用這個句型造句，描述一次你對某事的預期與實際不符的經驗。
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: I thought I had a clear picture of university life, but the reality was completely different.

sentence_patterns.pattern: Not [adverb] wrong, [adverb] wrong
sentence_patterns.meaning: 不是某種程度的錯誤，而是另一種程度的錯誤
sentence_patterns.example: We were wrong. Not a little wrong, almost entirely wrong.
sentence_patterns.template: Not [a little/slightly/partially] wrong, [completely/totally/almost entirely] wrong.
sentence_patterns.drill: 用這個句型強調一次重大的誤判。
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: I was wrong about the exam. Not a little wrong, completely wrong.

sentence_patterns.pattern: We came back with more [noun] than [noun]
sentence_patterns.meaning: 我們帶回了比某物更多的某物
sentence_patterns.example: We came back with more questions than answers.
sentence_patterns.template: [Subject] came back with more [questions/doubts/problems] than [answers/solutions/results].
sentence_patterns.drill: 用這個句型描述一次發人深省的旅行經歷。
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: After the trip, I came back with more curiosity than certainty about the culture.

sentence_patterns.pattern: If you only [verb], you will miss [noun]
sentence_patterns.meaning: 如果你只做某事，你會錯過某事
sentence_patterns.example: If you only watch the pretty pictures, you will miss the whole story.
sentence_patterns.template: If you only [focus on / look at / pay attention to] [superficial aspect], you will miss [deeper meaning / the real point].
sentence_patterns.drill: 用這個句型說明為什麼只看表面是不夠的。
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: If you only visit tourist attractions, you will miss the real life of the city.

quiz.question: How long did Tresa and Zarko travel through China?
quiz.explanation: The video states they spent 22 days traveling China privately.

quiz.question: Where did Zarko grow up?
quiz.explanation: Zarko grew up in Bulgaria under communism.

quiz.question: According to the video, what was the best possible outcome of the trip?
choices: Coming back with more questions than answers
quiz.explanation: Tresa says they came back with more questions than answers, which is 'the best possible outcome from any trip.'

quiz.question: At what age did Zarko leave Bulgaria?
quiz.explanation: Zarko left Bulgaria at age 25 as a political refugee.

quiz.question: What warning does the video give to viewers?
choices: Don't travel to China without a guide
If you only watch the pretty pictures, you will miss the whole story
China is too expensive for budget travelers
You need at least a month to see China
quiz.explanation: The narrator warns: 'If you only watch the pretty pictures, you will miss the whole story.'

_transcript_clean:
We had a theory about China. We had read
things, watched the news, talked to
people who had been there. We thought we
had a reasonable picture of what to
expect.
We were wrong. Not a little wrong,
almost entirely wrong. This is the first
video in a series about 22 days inside
China.
We traveled privately, just the two of
us, with a different local guide in each
city.
No tour buses, no group schedules, just
us moving through one of the most
extraordinary countries on earth trying
our best to understand it. We are going
to show you the landscapes and the food
and the things you come to China to see.
But we are also going to try to explain
something harder. What this country
actually is, how it thinks, what it has
been through, why it ended up here.
Because if you only watch the pretty
pictures, you will miss the whole story.
My name is Tresa. That guy you keep
seeing on screen exploring everything,
sitting in every car, that's my husband,
Zarko.
Together, we run this channel and
together we spent 22 days traveling
China privately.
We came back with more questions than
answers, which honestly is the best
possible outcome from any trip.
I'm American, born and raised in the US,
which means I grew up with a pretty
specific picture of the world.
Communism was the enemy. The Soviet
block was gray and scary.
And China was this distant, mysterious
place you only heard about when
something went wrong.
Zarko's story is different. He grew up
in Bulgaria under communism. Not reading
about it, living it. one party, state
control over everything. You kept your
head down and you did not ask the wrong
questions.
He left at 25 as a political refugee,
built a business in Seattle that
supported 125 families. And when we
retired, we moved back to Bulgaria to
Burgus on the Black Sea. That was 7
years ago.
I'm telling you all of this because it
matters for everything you're about to
see. When I look at China, I'm looking
through American eyes. When Zarko looks
at China, he's looking through the eyes
of someone who actually lived inside a
communist system.
Our questions are completely different.
And when we compare notes at the end of
the day, neither one of us has the full
picture alone, but together we get a
little closer to something real. That's
what this series is about. Getting
closer to something real about China.
Before we left, we spent weeks trying to
prepare,
reading, watching documentaries,
talking to anyone who'd actually been
there. And we kept coming back to the
same question. How
how does a country running a communist
system, the same basic system that gave
Bulgaria empty shelves,
that gave the Soviet Union the goologs,
that gave the world North Korea?
How does that same system produce the
world's second largest economy? Bullet
trains, cashless cities, more new
billionaires every year than anywhere
else on the planet. It didn't add up.
And for Zarko, who actually lived inside
that system, it made even less sense
than it did for me. So, either China was
doing something very different from what
the textbooks say, or the textbooks were
missing something important. We booked
22 days to find out.
We flew out of Istanbul. Turkish
Airlines is practically on our doorstep
from Burgus. And Istanbul has become one
of the great travel hubs in the world,
connected to almost everywhere. With
business class fairs that are a fraction
of what you'd pay on a European carrier.
On a 10-hour flight, business class
stops being a luxury. It becomes
self-preservation.
We landed early morning at Shanghai
Puong. And for our very first experience
of China, we decided to take the magv
into the city. Magnetic levitation, no
wheels on the track, top speed of 430
kmh.
It felt like exactly the right way to
arrive.
Honest review, it's 20 years old. The
technology is actually German, invented
in Germany, built here, then largely
abandoned worldwide because it was too
expensive and too inflexible to scale.
The train was nearly empty on a weekday
morning. The interior felt dated, and
the top speed we actually hit was around
300 km hour, which sounds impressive
until you learn that China's regular
high-speed trains now reach similar
speeds across the entire country. We'd
see that for ourselves on our first
bullet train ride a few days later. Our
hotel was right on Nanjing Road, not
near it, on it. We opened the curtains
and there it was, directly below us. The
most famous commercial street in
Shanghai, running from the bun straight
through the heart of the city. It never
stops, day or night. We slept for a few
hours. The 7-hour time difference from
Bulgaria is not kind. And then we went
walking.
This is the moment, the one I'd heard
other travelers talk about but hadn't
quite believed.
when what you expected and what you're
actually standing inside quietly come
apart.
The streets are spotless. The
architecture is extraordinary.
Every luxury brand you've ever heard of
is here
alongside Chinese brands I'd never
encountered.
People are well-dressed, unhurried,
going about their lives. Everything
moves with this purposeful efficiency. I
can only describe as organized.
People compare Nanjing Road at night to
Time Square. I've been to Time Square.
Time Square is loud and chaotic in a way
that feels almost accidental.
Nanjing Road feels deliberate, like
someone asked what a great city street
should actually look like and then built
the answer.
There's a robot that makes coffee right
there on the street. You walk up, tap
your order on a screen, and a mechanical

We walked to the Bund as the sun was
going down, the famous waterfront

Puong rising on the other.
It's one of the iconic images of modern
China.
>> The old world that came here and built
something and the new world that rose to
completely dwarf it. The crowd was
enormous and almost entirely Chinese
families, young couples, older people in
groups, very few Western faces. That
turned out to be a pattern for all 22
days.
China has become a massive internal
tourism destination.
>> The next morning, we had something
unusual planned.
We wanted to understand how China thinks
about technology because even before we
arrived, that felt like one of the keys
to understanding the country itself.
So, our guide took us to the Huawei
flagship store. In the West, Huawei is
complicated. In the US, the products are
outright banned, considered a national
security risk.
In Europe, there's ongoing debate.
And then you walk into their flagship
store in Shanghai and you immediately
understand why they make governments
nervous.
This is not a phone company. Huawei is
an entire ecosystem.
Smartphones, tablets, laptops,
smartwatches, home automation, and cars.
Full electric vehicles, multiple models,
beautiful things. Zarko moved through
that showroom like a kid the week before
Christmas. He sat in every single one.
The ST9, the Z7, the M9, every one of
them.
What stopped us both was a laptop called
the Matebook Fold.
It opens like a normal laptop.
Then the screen unfolds into a fulls
size display.
Something between a tablet and a
workstation in a single piece of
hardware.
The engineering is genuinely remarkable.
Zarko stood at that table for a long
time.
Then the Xiaomi store. Xiaomi makes
everything. Phones, TVs, rice cookers,
scooters, robot vacuums,
and a car. The Xiaomi SU7 is an electric
sedan that's been making European and
American automakers quietly nervous.
It looks better than most of what it
competes with,
and it costs considerably less.
the DJI store. Everything you're
watching right now was filmed on a DJI
Osmo Pocket 4. Zarko's camera of choice
for this channel. Walking into the DJI
flagship felt a little ceremonial.
Walking store to store that morning,
something kept pulling at me. This isn't
innovation arriving from nowhere. China
graduates more engineers every year than
any other country on Earth. The
government identified AI, electric
vehicles, and advanced manufacturing as
national priorities and then sustained
that investment for decades. Whatever
you think of the political system behind
those decisions, the output is sitting
right in front of you and it works.
>> You can go 1,000 km on this 1,400
hybrid.
One of the most remarkable things we saw
in Shanghai wasn't a landmark or a tech
showroom. It was a supermarket. Hearesh,
owned by Alibaba, looks like an upscale
grocery at first. Seafood tanks where
you point at your fish and they prepare
it for you. Beautiful produce, a hot
food section, normal aisles. But look
up. Running above the entire store is a
conveyor belt. A whole network of
ceiling tracks with bags hanging from
them, moving in every direction. A
silent river of orders flowing overhead
while people shop below. HEA isn't just
a supermarket. It's a fulfillment center
that also lets people shop in person.
Employees walk the aisles filling online
orders in real time. bag them, hook them
onto the overhead system, and the bags
travel to the back where delivery riders
are waiting 30 minutes or less within 3
km.
We were told some people specifically
choose where to live based on whether
they're inside a HEA delivery zone. The
delivery infrastructure across Shanghai
is unlike anything I'd seen before. In
the financial district at lunchtime, we
stop to watch the office lunch system in
action. Smart lockers built right into
building lobbies. A driver arrives, taps
a code, drops a bag of food in a
specific compartment. The person
upstairs gets a notification on WeChat,
comes down, opens their compartment,
takes their lunch. No contact, no
confusion. While we were standing there,
a drone arrived. an actual drone
descending from above the buildings with
a food bag hanging underneath it,
landing precisely on a marked drop zone.
The food came from a restaurant on the
rooftop of a nearby mall. From the
moment someone placed the order to the
moment the drone landed, 12 minutes.
Our guide told us her mother, an elderly
woman living alone, gets almost
everything delivered this way.
groceries, meals, household items. She
orders on WeChat, pays on WeChat, and it
arrives at her door. She never has to
carry heavy bags or fight through
crowded markets. I found that genuinely
moving. And that's when I started to
understand why everyone in China is on
their phone. It isn't mindless
scrolling. Their phone isn't a
distraction from their life. It is the
system that runs their life. And in
China, it runs it remarkably well.
We also spent time in the BYD showroom.
Build your dreams.
Now the largest electric vehicle
manufacturer in the world by sales.
Bigger than Tesla.
Bigger than Volkswagen.
In China, electric vehicles aren't a
trend or a glimpse of the future.
They're just what cars are.
One of the malls we visited had car
dealerships on the ground floor. Inside
the shopping mall, not in the parking
lot, between the restaurant and the
clothing store. You could buy an
electric vehicle and then go up two
floors for dinner. The contrast with the
Tesla showroom nearby was hard to
ignore. Huawei had eight or nine models
filling the floor. And Neo is similar.
multiple models, lounge atmosphere,
salespeople who genuinely wanted to walk
you through everything. Tesla had two
cars. It felt sparse by comparison.
On our last morning in Shanghai, our
guide did something we weren't
expecting.
Instead of another landmark or another
showroom, she took us to a park. It was
early, maybe , in the morning,
and the park was full. Not full like a
tourist site. Full of people who belong
there. Retired people mostly spread
across the whole space in different
groups. Each one doing something
completely different.
>> What is the name of this park?
>> Pushing Park.
>> Pushing Park. Pushing Park.
>> Pushing Park.
>> Those are for disabled people.
>> Wow.
>> Tai Chi in one corner. a ballroom
dancing group nearby. 20 people partner
dancing on the paving stones to music
from a small speaker on a bench.
Further along, a group of musicians
playing traditional instruments
together.
And then further still, a group just
singing together like they did this
every single morning.
I joined the Tai Chi group. It looked
deceptively easy, slow, balanced,
graceful. But the moment I tried to
follow along, I realized I had
completely misjudged it. Balancing on
one foot, moving through those
sequences, it takes real muscle, real
concentration, real practice. Tai Chi is
not what it looks like from the outside.
We spent almost two hours there. We
hadn't planned to. We just couldn't
leave. At some point, our guide
mentioned that women in China retire at
57. Men around 62 or 63, earlier than
almost anywhere in the Western world.
And this is what they do with that time.
every morning together, not at home in
front of a television, out in the fresh
air, moving, playing, singing,
connecting.
Zaro said something afterwards that I
keep coming back to. He said, "This is
what retirement should look like." I
couldn't argue with him. After those
first few days in Shanghai, one question
kept coming back. We'd walked Nanjing
Road, stood at the Bund, watched a drone
deliver lunch in 12 minutes, danced with
strangers in a park at in the
morning. How did this happen? Here's
what I knew going in. In 1950, China was
one of the poorest countries on Earth.
The average person here earned less than
the average person in subsaharan Africa.
A century of war, invasion, famine, and
political upheaval had torn the country
apart. No electricity in most villages,
no paved roads, no real industry. And
then it got worse before it got better.
In 1958, Mao Zaong launched what he
called the Great Leap Forward. The plan
was to transform China into a modern
industrial power. Within 15 years,
private farming was abolished.
The people at home had nothing left to
eat. Between 15 and 45 million people
died in the famine that followed.
This is the China that existed within
living memory.
Many of the older people we met on this
trip, guides, hotel staff, people who
looked roughly our age, were born into
that world, or their parents were. Real
hollow stomach starvation isn't distant
history here. It's one generation back.
Then in 1978, something changed. Mao had
died two years earlier. A man named Dung
Xiaoing took power. And Dung looked at
the wreckage of the previous 30 years
and made a decision that was remarkable
for how simple it was. Whatever works,
he said, if a cat catches mice, it
doesn't matter if it's black or white.
He opened China to foreign investment,
allowed private business alongside the
state, created special zones where
market capitalism was explicitly
permitted just to test it, to see what
happened. He sent Chinese students
abroad to study in western universities.
He rewarded results. He kept the
communist party in political control,
kept the one party system, the state
surveillance, the control of
information. But onto all of that he
grafted a form of capitalism so
aggressive and competitive. It makes
most western economies look cautious.
And the result, China today is not
communist in any way Markx would
recognize. It's something with no clean
name. A one party political structure
running on top of an extraordinarily
dynamic market economy.
The government sets direction. The
market does the work. The people get
richer and in exchange for that rising
prosperity, they accept a level of state
control over their political lives and
their access to information that would
be unthinkable in a western democracy.
From Shanghai, we flew south to Guin, a
landscape so ancient it's been appearing
in Chinese paintings for 2,000 years. We
cruised the Lee River. We climbed into
the rice terraces of Lji
and we started to understand that China
is not one thing. It's many things at
once and they don't always agree with
each other. Over the next videos, we'll
take you through 11 cities and 22 days.
Landscapes unlike anything in Europe.
People who were warmer and more generous
than either of us had any reason to
expect.
And we'll try to understand without
looking away from the complicated parts
what China actually is.

title: 4 Days in Italy's Best-Kept Secret: The Puglia Region
summary: 4 Days in Italy's Best-Kept Secret: The Puglia Region

english.heading: Part 1
english.text:
After three full days in Bari,
Polignano, Monopoli,
Matera,
Altamura,
we thought we understood this region. We
thought we had a feel for it.
>> 
>> The limestone towns, the cobblestone
streets,
the rhythm of Italian life slowing to
something almost human.
And then Lecce happened. Lecce sits at
the very bottom of the  heel of
Italy's boot.
Further south than most people think to
go.
The kind of place that doesn't 
appear on the quick itinerary. And for
that reason alone, it rewards 
the people who make the effort.
We gave it four days.
And four days turned  out to be
just barely enough.
Getting to Lecce was simple enough.
A train from  Bari, comfortable,
easy. The countryside rolling past the
window. Dry stone walls, olive trees,
the flat open plain  of Salento,
stretching out in every direction.
But arriving in Lecce, that's where the
story gets honest. We were following

center.
And I'll be straight with you.
The first few minutes were not exactly
what we expected. The road into town was
rough.
Partially broken asphalt, a little

did we make a mistake? And gradually,

We're here for four nights.

Check out this view.
Right here, right in front of the

And the center.
They're

Archaeological. They're excavating an

So,  this is We're directly in

Even the bathroom  had a touch

toilet, too.
This is our home base for the south of
Puglia.
Lecce carries a nickname that took us
 a while to fully understand.
They call it the Florence of the South.
It doesn't look like Florence. It
doesn't really. What happened here
architecturally 
between 1630 and the end of the 1700s so
unusually that historians ran out of
comparisons  and reached for the
greatest city they knew. Florence.
After the Battle of Lepanto in 1571
when the Christian navies defeated the
Ottoman fleet the people of Salento
finally for the first time in
generations felt safe from invasion. The
Turkish raids  that had
terrorized this coastline for decades
suddenly stopped. And when people
finally feel safe they build. New
religious orders arrived  in
Lecce. The Jesuits the Theatines and
they began competing with each other
through architecture.
Who could build the more impressive
church? Who could commission the more
elaborate 
facade?
What made Lecce's version of
architecture completely unique was one
thing, the stone. Pietra Leccese
>> 
>> Lecce stone.
A local limestone so soft that a
sculptor could carve it almost like
wood. It hardens over time when exposed
 to air. But it could be worked
with extraordinary precision.
The Roman amphitheater 
in the center of town was discovered by
accident in 1901
while workers were digging the
foundation for  a new building.
Underneath the piazza is an amphitheater
with seating for 20,000 people.
What we see today is only a small
portion.
The rest is still buried beneath the
square. The shops of the city.
An entire Roman city still lies largely
undisturbed just beneath our feet.
Piazza Sant'Oronzo is where the
amphitheater sits. And the column of
Saint Oronzo rises. Cafes line the edges
of the square.
Lecce is a living city with 2,000 years
of history. The mosaic emblem of Lecce
right in the center of the piazza, the
city's symbol depicts Lecce's coat of
arms, a wolf beneath an oak tree.
Of all the churches in Lecce's historic
 center, the one that surprised
us most quietly is the Church of Santa
Chiara. From the outside, elaborate.

From the inside, equally elaborate.
But what everything was actually made
of? Papier-mâché. 
Every carved detail, every gilded frame,
every surface that looked like wood or
carved stone,
>> 
>> all of it, papier-mâché.
Made by craftsmen who had neither the
money for marble nor the stone for
carving, 
and so they invented their own
tra

english.heading: Part 3
english.text:
techniques, making figures and
decorations in the same tradition. It is
a craft born from poverty that 
became an art form.
We have been to a lot of churches. We
have seen a lot of facades. By this
point in our travels, we have developed
something close to church fatigue.
That point where one elaborate entrance
begins to blur  into the next.
Santa Croce does not allow for that.

It demands your attention whether you
give it willingly or not.
The facade is overwhelming. A rose
window so intricately carved, 
it seems physically impossible to have
been made by hand. Around it, a dense,
exuberant world of carved 
figures. Saints, animals, mythological
creatures, angels, decorative
vegetables, triumphal imagery
celebrating the Christian victory at
Lepanto. It goes on and on.
Every time  you think you've
taken it all in, you notice something
new. It took over a hundred years to
build. 100 years of stonemasons adding
details to  details, passing the
work from generation to generation.
A short walk from the amphitheater
through the narrow main walking street
is the Piazza del Duomo.
Surrounded on all sides by Baroque
buildings. The cathedral on one side,
the bishop's palace on another, the
seminary with its magnificent loggia on
the third.
The space feels almost theatrical.
As if it was designed not just as
 a place to put a church, but as
a place to make an impression. The bell
tower is the tallest in all of Salento.
It stands 70 m tall.
And as of 2022, there is an elevator. We
are glad not to have to climb the
stairs.
From the top, you see the full geometry
of the old city laid out below you.
The tight medieval street plan, the
Baroque churches, and then beyond the
old walls, the modern city of Lecce
spreading out in every direction.
It is a good place to take a breath and
understand where we are.
Inside the museum next door stopped us
in a way we hadn't expected. The statue
that caught our eye immediately, a woman
holding a severed head, composed, even
serene. The kind of image that takes a
moment  to fully register.
This is Judith from the Old Testament,
the Jewish widow who beheaded Assyrian
general with his own sword. Standing
next to her in the same  room was
a painting of a woman holding a chisel
above a man's head while another figure
raised a hammer to strike it. That was a
harder image to  shake. Medieval
art did not look away from suffering the
way modern sensibility prefers to.
Outside the historic center, 
Porta Napoli, the great triumphal arch
that

english.heading: Part 4
english.text:
s
 simply a functioning Italian
city. Supermarkets, traffic, pharmacies,
the kind of ordinary daily life that can
feel like a relief after so many hours
of looking  at extraordinary
things.
We used Lecce as our base for the
surrounding region, the same way we'd
use Bari before it. And the first day
trip
>> 
>> took us to a town that almost nobody
outside of Italy has heard of,
Galatina.
Quiet, small,
>> 
>> not many tourists.
We like those places. The less touristy
experience, the more we like  it.
We feel we are actually somewhere.
What brought us to Galatina was the
Basilica of Santa Caterina.
>> 
>> And from the outside, we would not have
predicted what was waiting for us
inside.
Every centimeter of the interior is
covered in frescoes. 
Your eyes don't know where to go.
Walls, ceiling, 
vaults, columns, everything painted top
to bottom in scenes from the Bible,
the life  of St. Catherine, the
Book of Revelation, the story of
creation. It is like someone took the
entire illustrated Bible 
and put it inside a single room, all
painted in the 15th century.
And then we stopped  at one
fresco in particular, a scene showing a
woman's breast  being cut.
Graphic, arresting, impossible to simply
walk past. This is Saint  Agatha
of Sicily, one of the early Christian
martyrs who was tortured in exactly this
way for refusing to renounce her faith.
In the center  of town, we
stopped for the leche coffee, poured
over ice with a splash of almond milk
syrup, a drink so particular to this
corner of Italy. We're trying the local
um coffee here and the local pastry. And
this is a cool coffee, but it has almond
milk.
It's kind of  sweet, but it
almost tastes like an almond liqueur,
actually, not like almond milk. The
pastries here are from this region and
one is cherry 
and the other one is cream. Here you go,
so let's
see the cherry  one.
Oh, look at that. It has like a
lemon filling,
>> 
>> like a pudding.
Let's see how it tastes.
Yes, it's good.
It feels amazing. So, it's like very
light.
Next, we're going to the Adriatic, to
the historic town of Otranto. Otranto
captured our hearts.
It sits on the Adriatic, the easternmost
town in Italy, the point of land that
faces Albania across the water.
And from the moment we walked into the
old town, through the walls, down toward
the water,
>> 
>> we started thinking about coming back
for a longer stay. The town has a Greek
quality to it. The white walls, the
narrow streets, the sea visible from
everywhere. That feeling makes sen

english.heading: Part 5
english.text:
mbining
the Old Testament, the life of Christ,
King Arthur, Alexander the Great,
mythological animals, the signs of the
zodiac, and scenes of daily medieval
life.
The frescoes that were painted here were
 destroyed when the Ottomans
came. They used the cathedral as a
mosque. That invasion happened in 1480.
150 ships carrying 18,000 soldiers
landed on this coast.
The city resisted for 2 weeks.
When the walls finally fell, 
the women and children were enslaved or
killed.
The men who survived, 813 of them, were
taken to a hill outside the city
>> 
>> and given a choice.
Convert to Islam or die. Those 813 men,
their bones are in the cathedral.
>> 
>> In the Chapel of the Martyrs, and seven
large glass cases display the skulls and
bones.
They are arranged carefully,
deliberately, 
facing outward.
Down to the waterfront. It is windy, but
the light on the water  is
beautiful. We can imagine this place in
summer, full, alive, 
perhaps too full.
From Otranto, we drove south along the
dramatic coastline.
Sharp limestone cliffs dropping straight
into the Adriatic.
And every few kilometers along the cliff
edge,  a tower.
These watchtowers, dozens of them, still
partially standing,
>> 
>> built between the 15th and 17th
centuries, were the early warning system
of this coast. 
Each tower positioned so it was visible
from the next one along the shore,
creating a chain of signals.
We passed through Castro, a small
dramatic town perched directly above the
sea, and drove all the way to Santa
Maria di Leuca, the very tip of the
heel, the southernmost point of Puglia,
the place where the Adriatic and the
Ionian Sea meet.
Making our way back toward Lecce along
the Ionian coast, we stopped in
Gallipoli,
>> 
>> a medieval fishing town built almost
entirely on an island, connected to the
mainland by a single  bridge. We
walked the full loop of the old town
walls, looking out over the Ionian Sea.
Then down the main street lined with
shops and restaurants, still quiet in
April compared to what it becomes in
July.
Fishing boats in the harbor, the smell
of the sea in the wind.
Puglia, it is not a flashy region. It
doesn't have Rome's command  or
Tuscany's fame. It doesn't try to
overwhelm you.
What it does instead 
is accumulate.
A bakery that has been making bread in
the same  oven for 900 years, an
amphitheater buried under a city center.
Puglia rewards the decision to stay a
little longer than you planned, to take
the train  to the small town
nobody mentioned.
If you enjoyed what

chinese.heading: 第一部分
chinese.text: 在巴里、波利尼亞諾、莫諾波利、馬泰拉和阿爾塔穆拉度過了整整三天之後，我們以為自己已經了解這個地區了。我們以為自己對這裡有了感覺——石灰岩小鎮、鵝卵石街道、義大利生活節奏放慢到近乎人性的步調。然後萊切出現了。萊切坐落在義大利靴子後跟的最底部——比大多數人願意去的地方還要更南。它是那種不會出現在短期行程中的地方。正因為這個原因，它會回報那些願意付出努力的人。我們給了它四天的時間。結果四天只是勉強夠用。前往萊切很簡單：從巴里搭火車，舒適又方便。鄉村景色從車窗外掠過——乾石牆、橄欖樹、薩倫托平坦開闊的平原向四面八方延伸。但抵達萊切的時候，故事才開始變得真實。我們拖著行李，依賴Google Maps步行前往歷史中心。我跟你直說吧——最初的幾分鐘完全不是我們預期的樣子。進城的路很粗糙——部分破損的柏油路、牆上有些塗鴉、商店不多、沒什麼魅力。我記得自己當時在想：我們是不是搞錯了？然後漸漸地，幾乎沒有一個明確的轉變時刻，事情開始發生變化。柏油路變成了鵝卵石。幾家精品店出現了。街道變窄，變成了某種美麗的古樸風貌。然後視野突然開闊了——一個寬闊的廣場、一座紀念碑，就在歷史中心的中央，一座羅馬圓形劇場——不是複製品，不是重建品，而是真正的石砌座位，兩千年的歷史，坐落在一個活生生的義大利城市中心，就像它本來就屬於那裡一樣。我們的公寓就在那裡，面向著圓形劇場。有些地點是你無法計劃的——你只能靠運氣。午安。我們剛到萊切，你——

chinese.heading: 第二部分
chinese.text: 一個開關。連浴室都有觸控面板控制水，沒錯，馬桶也是。這是我們在普利亞南部的根據地。萊切有一個別名，我們花了一段時間才完全理解——人們稱它為「南方的佛羅倫斯」。它看起來不像佛羅倫斯——真的不像。但在1630年到18世紀末之間，這裡發生的建築成就如此非凡，以至於歷史學家用盡了比較對象，最終只能想到他們所知道的最偉大的城市：佛羅倫斯。1571年勒班陀戰役之後，基督教海軍擊敗了鄂圖曼艦隊，薩倫托的人民幾代人以來第一次感到不受侵略的威脅。數十年來一直困擾這片海岸的土耳其突襲突然停止了。當人們終於感到安全的時候，他們就會開始建造。新的修會來到萊切——耶穌會、戴蒂尼會——它們開始透過建築互相競爭：誰能建造更宏偉的教堂？誰能委託創作更精緻的外牆？讓萊切版本的建築完全獨一無二的只有一件事——石材。Pietra Leccese——萊切石。這是一種當地的石灰岩，質地柔軟到雕刻家可以像雕刻木材一樣雕刻它，但在接觸空氣後會隨著時間的推移而硬化。它可以被加工得異常精細。城中心的羅馬圓形劇場是在1901年意外發現的——當時工人正在為一棟新建築挖掘地基。廣場下方是一座可容納兩萬人的圓形劇場。我們今天看到的只是一小部分，其餘部分仍然埋藏在廣場和城市建築的下方。一整座羅馬城市仍然幾乎完好無損地躺在我們腳下。圓形劇場所在的聖奧龍佐廣場上矗立著聖奧龍佐紀念柱，咖啡館沿著廣場邊緣排列。萊切是一座擁有兩千年歷史的活生生的城市。萊切的馬賽克標誌就在——

chinese.heading: 第三部分
chinese.text: 技術，以同樣的傳統製作人像和裝飾。這是一門從貧困中誕生、最終成為藝術形式的手藝。我們去過很多教堂，見過很多外牆——到目前為止，我們已經發展出某種接近「教堂疲勞」的狀態，也就是一座華麗的入口開始模糊成下一座的那個臨界點。但聖十字教堂不允許你這樣。它強行索取你的注意力，不管你願不願意。它的外牆令人震撼。玫瑰窗雕刻得如此精緻繁複，以至於看起來幾乎不可能是手工製作的。在它周圍，是一個密集而繁茂的雕刻世界——聖徒、動物、神話生物、天使、裝飾性植物、慶祝基督教在勒班陀勝利的凱旋意象——層層疊疊，無窮無盡。每次你以為自己已經看完了全部，你又會注意到新的東西。它花了一百多年才建成——一百年的石匠在細節上添加細節，將作品一代一代地傳承下去。從圓形劇場步行不遠，穿過狹窄的主要步行街，就到了大教堂廣場。廣場四面被巴洛克建築環繞——一側是大教堂，另一側是主教宮，第三側是擁有宏偉涼廊的神學院。這個空間幾乎像劇場一樣——彷彿它不只是為了安置一座教堂而設計，而是為了給人留下深刻印象。鐘樓是整個薩倫托最高的，高達70公尺。從2022年開始，這裡有了電梯——我們很慶幸不用爬樓梯。從頂端俯瞰，你可以看到舊城區的完整幾何結構在你腳下展開——緊湊的中世紀街道規劃、巴洛克教堂，然後越過舊城牆，現代的萊切市區向四面八方延伸。這是一個停下來喘口氣、理解我們身在何處的好地方。隔壁博物館的內部以一種我們意想不到的方式讓我們停下了腳步。引起我們注意的那尊雕像——

chinese.heading: 第四部分
chinese.text: 它只是一個正常運作的義大利城市——超市、交通、藥局，那種平凡的日常生活，在花了那麼多小時欣賞非凡事物之後，反而讓人感到放鬆。我們以萊切為據點探索周邊地區，就像之前以巴里為據點一樣。第一天的行程帶我們去了一個幾乎沒有義大利以外的人聽說過的小鎮——加拉蒂納。安靜、小巧、遊客不多。我們喜歡這樣的地方——越不觀光化的體驗，我們越喜歡。我們覺得自己真的身在某個地方。把我們帶到加拉蒂納的是聖凱瑟琳大教堂。從外面看，我們完全無法預測裡面有什麼在等著我們。內部每一公分都覆蓋著壁畫。你的眼睛不知道該往哪裡看——牆壁、天花板、拱頂、柱子，從上到下全部彩繪著聖經場景、聖凱瑟琳的生平、啟示錄、創世記的故事。就像有人把一整本插圖聖經放進了一個房間裡——全部繪於15世紀。然後我們在一幅特定的壁畫前停了下來——一個描繪女性乳房被割下的場景。畫面寫實、令人震驚，讓人無法就這麼走過去。這是西西里的聖阿加莎——早期基督教殉道者之一，因拒絕放棄信仰而遭受了這樣的酷刑。在鎮中心，我們停下來品嚐了萊切咖啡——加冰沖泡，配上一點杏仁奶糖漿，是義大利這個角落特有的飲料。我們來試試當地的咖啡和當地的糕點。這是一種冰咖啡，但加了杏仁奶——它有點甜，但實際上嚐起來像杏仁利口酒，不像杏仁奶。這裡的糕點是本地特產——一個是櫻桃口味，另一個是奶油口味。來吧，嚐嚐這個櫻桃的——哦，你看，裡面有檸檬餡——

chinese.heading: 第五部分
chinese.text: 融合了舊約聖經、基督生平、亞瑟王、亞歷山大大帝、神話動物、黃道十二宮符號和中世紀日常生活場景。曾經繪製在這裡的壁畫在鄂圖曼人來臨時被摧毀了——他們把大教堂當作清真寺使用。那次入侵發生在1480年：150艘船搭載著18,000名士兵登陸在這片海岸。城市抵抗了兩週。當城牆最終淪陷時，婦女和兒童被奴役或殺害。倖存的男性——813人——被帶到城外的一座小山上，面臨一個選擇：改信伊斯蘭教，或者死。這813名男性——他們的遺骨就在大教堂裡。在殉道者禮拜堂裡，七個大型玻璃陳列櫃展示著他們的頭骨和骨骼。它們被精心而刻意地排列著，面向外方。一路走到海邊。風很大，但水面上的光線很美。我們可以想像這裡夏天的樣子——熙熙攘攘、充滿生機，也許太過擁擠了。從奧特朗托出發，我們沿著壯麗的海岸線向南行駛——尖銳的石灰岩懸崖直插入亞得里亞海。沿著懸崖邊緣，每隔幾公里就有一座塔樓。這些瞭望塔——數十座，部分仍然矗立——建於15至17世紀，是這片海岸的早期預警系統。每座塔樓的位置都能被海岸線上的下一座塔樓看到，形成了一條信號鏈。我們經過了卡斯特羅——一座直接懸在海面上的小型戲劇性城鎮——一路開到了聖瑪利亞迪萊烏卡，靴子的最尖端、普利亞的最南端、亞得里亞海和愛奧尼亞海交匯的地方。沿著愛奧尼亞海岸返回萊切的路上，我們在加利波利停留——一座幾乎完全建在島上的中世紀漁村，由一座橋樑與大陸相連。我們沿著舊城牆走了一圈，眺望愛奧尼亞海，然後——

collocations.meaning: 鮮為人知的秘密；隱藏的好去處
collocations.example: Lecce is Italy's best-kept secret in the Puglia region.
collocations.example_zh: Lecce是義大利Puglia地區鮮為人知的秘密景點。
collocations.practice: This small town is a ______ that few tourists know about.

collocations.example: Lecce rewards the people who make the effort to visit.
collocations.example_zh: Lecce會回報那些付出努力前往的人。
collocations.practice: The hike was difficult, but the view at the top ______.

collocations.example: Four days turned out to be just barely enough.
collocations.example_zh: 結果四天的時間只是勉強夠用。
collocations.practice: One hour was ______ to see the entire museum.

collocations.example: I'll be straight with you — the first few minutes were not what we expected.
collocations.example_zh: 我跟你直說——前幾分鐘完全不是我們預期的樣子。
collocations.practice: ______, this restaurant is not as good as people say.

collocations.example: The asphalt gave way to cobblestone streets.
collocations.example_zh: 柏油路變成了鵝卵石街道。
collocations.practice: As we walked further, the modern buildings ______ historic houses.

collocations.example: A Roman amphitheater, right in the middle of the historic center.
collocations.example_zh: 一座羅馬圓形劇場，就在歷史中心的正中央。
collocations.practice: There's a beautiful fountain ______ the square.

collocations.example: The kind of place that doesn't appear on the quick itinerary.
collocations.example_zh: 那種不會出現在簡短行程中的地方。
collocations.practice: This café is ______ makes you want to stay all afternoon.

collocations.meaning: 故事開始變得真實；不再修飾
collocations.example: Arriving in Lecce, that's where the story gets honest.
collocations.example_zh: 抵達Lecce時，故事才開始變得真實。
collocations.practice: When I saw the actual living conditions, ______.

grammar.point: Past Simple vs Past Continuous for Scene Setting
grammar.explanation: 簡單過去式和過去進行式結合使用，用於描述背景和發生的具體事件。過去進行式（was/were + verb-ing）用來設置場景，而簡單過去式則用於描述在該背景下發生的具體事件。

examples.from_text: We were following Google Maps on foot, pulling our luggage, heading toward the historic center.
examples.analysis: 使用過去進行式（were following, pulling, heading）來描述當時正在進行的連續動作，營造出畫面感。
examples.pattern: Subject + was/were + verb-ing... (background action)

examples.from_text: We thought we had a feel for it. And then Lecce happened.
examples.analysis: 'Lecce happened' 使用簡單過去式，表示一個突然發生的事件，打斷了之前的狀態。這種對比很有效。
examples.pattern: Subject + thought + clause... And then [event] happened.

grammar.practice: 將以下句子改寫，使用過去進行式設置背景：'We walked through the city. We saw a Roman amphitheater.'
grammar.practice_answer: 'We were walking through the city when we saw a Roman amphitheater.'

grammar.point: Gradual Transition Expressions
grammar.explanation: 表達逐漸變化的詞彙和句型，用於描述緩慢的轉變過程。影片中用 'gradually'、'almost without a clear moment of transition' 和 'things started to shift' 來強調變化的漸進性。

examples.from_text: And gradually, almost without a clear moment of transition, things started to shift.
examples.analysis: 使用 'gradually' 和 'almost without a clear moment of transition' 兩個詞組強調變化是緩慢且不易察覺的。'started to shift' 表示開始變化的過程。
examples.pattern: Gradually, almost without [a clear moment / a specific point] of [transition / change], [subject] started to [verb].

examples.from_text: The asphalt gave way to cobblestone. A few boutiques appeared. Then the streets narrowed into something beautifully old.
examples.analysis: 連續使用三個簡單過去式的句子來描述漸進的變化，每個句子都推進了一步，透過具體的細節（asphalt→cobblestone, boutiques appeared, streets narrowed）展現變化。
examples.pattern: [First thing] gave way to [second thing]. [New element] appeared. Then [subject] [verb + into] [new state].

grammar.practice: 使用 'gradually' 和 'started to shift' 描述一個城市從工業區轉變為藝術區的過程。
grammar.practice_answer: 'Gradually, almost without a clear moment of transition, the neighborhood started to shift. Factories gave way to galleries, and the streets filled with street art.'

sentence_patterns.pattern: [Subject] sits at the very [location] of [geographical feature]
sentence_patterns.meaning: 某物位於某地理特徵的最某處
sentence_patterns.example: Lecce sits at the very bottom of the heel of Italy's boot.
sentence_patterns.template: [City/Place] sits at the very [top/bottom/edge] of [region/peninsula/island].
sentence_patterns.drill: 用這個句型描述一個你熟悉的地理位置。
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: My hometown sits at the very edge of the Pacific coast.

sentence_patterns.pattern: Further [direction] than most people think to [verb]
sentence_patterns.meaning: 比大多數人認為的還要某方向
sentence_patterns.example: Further south than most people think to go.
sentence_patterns.template: Further [north/south/east/west] than most people think to [go/travel/venture].
sentence_patterns.drill: 用這個句型描述一個比較偏遠的地方。
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: The village is further into the mountains than most people think to go.

sentence_patterns.pattern: Did we make a mistake? And then, [event]
sentence_patterns.meaning: 我們是不是做錯了？然後...（轉折）
sentence_patterns.example: Did we make a mistake? And gradually, things started to shift.
sentence_patterns.template: Did I/we make a mistake? And [then/gradually], [positive turn of events].
sentence_patterns.drill: 用這個句型描述一個從懷疑到驚喜的轉變。
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: Did I make a mistake choosing this restaurant? And then, the food arrived and it was amazing.

sentence_patterns.pattern: Not a [noun], not a [noun] — [real thing]
sentence_patterns.meaning: 不是某物，不是某物——而是真品
sentence_patterns.example: Not a replica, not a reconstruction — the real thing.
sentence_patterns.template: Not a [fake/replica/copy], not a [model/imitation] — [the real thing / authentic version].
sentence_patterns.drill: 用這個句型強調某物是真品。
sentence_patterns.drill_answer: The antique shop sold not a reproduction, not a fake — the real Ming dynasty vase.

quiz.question: Where is Lecce located?
choices: At the very bottom of Italy's boot heel
quiz.explanation: Lecce sits at the very bottom of the heel of Italy's boot, in the Salento peninsula.

quiz.question: What did Zarko find at the entrance to Lecce?
choices: Rough roads with broken asphalt and graffiti
quiz.explanation: The road into Lecce had broken asphalt, graffiti, and not much charm at first.

quiz.question: What surprised them in the historic center of Lecce?
quiz.explanation: In the middle of the historic center, they found a Roman amphitheater — not a replica but the real thing.

quiz.question: How long did the couple spend in Lecce?
quiz.explanation: They gave Lecce four days, and four days turned out to be just barely enough.

quiz.question: What was the initial impression of Lecce like?
choices: Disappointing — they wanted to leave immediately
Rough at first but gradually improved
quiz.explanation: The first few minutes were rough with broken asphalt and graffiti, and Zarko wondered if they made a mistake.

english.text:
There are places that take you by
surprise.
Not because you didn't know they
existed, but because you thought you

You had the picture in your mind. You

everything you assumed dissolves.
That is Puglia.
We are going to spend time here,
genuinely exploring, not rushing, not

exactly why this particular corner of
Southern Italy has quietly become one of
the most talked about destinations in
all of Europe. But first, how we got
here
and why.
One of the things that we love most
about living in Burgas, Bulgaria, is a
feeling that took us a while to name.
When we lived in Seattle on the West

A flight to Hawaii, 6 hours. Mexico,
roughly the same.
Europe, most of a day with a connection.
You planned big trips months in advance
because every journey was a commitment.
Here, it's different, completely
different. Look at a map of the world
and find Bulgaria. It's just kind of at
a crossroads, close enough to the Middle
East,  to Asia, to all of Western
Europe, and even to Africa.
Istanbul, one of the fastest growing

And you can actually stand still for a
